content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6-max

## Summary

Reviewed the failure reconciliation implementation across 8 modified source files (~3500 lines of new code in failure_reconciliation.rs plus ~900 lines of changes across daemon_loop, controller, model, service, fs, run, and queries). All 21 failure-reconciliation-specific tests and all 999 library tests pass. Clippy clean with -D warnings, fmt check passes. The implementation correctly: records BeadFailed journal events, counts attempts via journal+lineage deduplication, escalates to NeedsOperator after MAX_FAILURE_RETRIES (3), handles idempotent replays, preserves bead state (no br close on failure), manages stale replay conflicts when newer retries exist, and transitions the controller through Blocked (retryable) or NeedsOperator (escalated). The state machine changes (Blocked now preserves active context, new Blocked→Running and Blocked→Reconciling transitions) are coherent with the retry semantics. No critical bugs, safety problems, or correctness gaps found.

## Amendments

1. repair_historical_failure_state() unconditionally returns Ok(true) regardless of whether the repair actually mutated state (failure_reconciliation.rs:718). This means the caller always calls repair_recorded_failure_attempt_ordinals even when no repair occurred. The ordinal repair function has its own skip-if-unchanged guard (failure_state_matches_expected), so this is not a correctness bug, but it performs unnecessary I/O (re-reading runs and journal) on every stale replay where the state was already correct. Consider having repair_task_run_with_disposition return whether it actually wrote changes, and propagating that.
   Rationale: Not a bug — the current code is correct because the ordinal repair has a skip guard. But for high-volume failure reconciliation (many stale replays during daemon catch-up), the unnecessary re-reads of task-runs.ndjson and journal.ndjson on every no-op repair add avoidable I/O. This is low severity since it only affects performance, not correctness.
